subsystem: ImageMagick JPEG 2000 coder — the WriteJP2Image() encoder that serializes an in-memory ImageMagick Image into a JP2/J2K/JPT stream using the OpenJPEG (libopenjp2) library. It configures opj_cparameters_t, builds an opj_image_t from the pixel data, and drives opj_start_compress/opj_encode/opj_end_compress.
provenance: Identified by the MagickCore idioms (MagickBooleanType, ImageInfo/Image/ExceptionInfo, MagickCoreSignature, GetImageOption, GetVirtualPixels, ThrowWriterException, LogMagickEvent) and the OpenJPEG API surface (opj_cparameters_t, opj_image_cmptparm_t, opj_image_create, opj_setup_encoder, opj_start_compress, OPJ_CLRSPC_*, OPJ_CODEC_JP2). The sample banner names corpus/train/train_0193_cwe665.c and the internal tag 'primevul_c_train_2700_fixed'; the function is coders/jp2.c:WriteJP2Image from ImageMagick.
trust_boundary: Two untrusted sources meet here. (1) The image content — image->columns, image->rows, image->depth, image->colorspace, image->alpha_trait — is decoded from an attacker-supplied input image (common in conversion/thumbnail services), so these drive buffer sizes, shift counts and loop bounds. (2) The ImageInfo option/property strings — GetImageOption("jp2:number-resolutions"/"jp2:quality"/"jp2:rate"/"jp2:progression-order"), image_info->extract, image_info->sampling_factor, image_info->quality and the image 'comment' property — are caller/metadata controlled and are parsed with StringToInteger/sscanf directly into fixed-size fields of the stack-resident opj_cparameters_t. Hostile fields: the comma-separated jp2:quality/jp2:rate lists, the number-resolutions integer, the sampling_factor pair, and the image geometry/depth.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The encoder entry point. It takes image_info (the write configuration and option bag), image (the source pixels and metadata to encode), and exception (the error sink). It returns a MagickBooleanType success flag. This is a WRITE path, so the 'attacker' surface is the already-decoded image (whose header fields came from an untrusted input file) plus the option strings the caller passes through image_info.

Local declarations. parameters is a stack-resident opj_cparameters_t — the OpenJPEG encoder configuration struct, which contains several FIXED-size inline arrays (notably tcp_rates[] and tcp_distoratio[], each 100 floats in openjpeg.h). jp2_info[5] is a fixed array of five per-component descriptors (opj_image_cmptparm_t) that will be filled per colour channel and handed to opj_image_create. The single register ssize_t i is REUSED as the loop counter for the resolution loop, both option-parsing loops, and the per-channel loops, so each loop must re-seed it. channels is the number of image components (1..4).

Standard MagickCore preamble: assert non-NULL, well-signed handles (asserts are compiled out in release builds, so they are documentation not enforcement), optional trace log, then OpenBlob opens the output destination for binary writing. If the blob cannot be opened it returns the failure status immediately. No untrusted parsing yet.

opj_set_default_encoder_parameters initializes the whole parameters struct to library defaults (including subsampling_dx/dy = 1). The loop derives a default wavelet-resolution count from the image dimensions: it walks i=1..5 and stops as soon as 2^(i+2) exceeds both columns and rows, then stores that i (clamped to at most 5 by the loop bound) into parameters.numresolution. This gives a sane in-range resolution count based on image size.

If the caller supplied a 'jp2:number-resolutions' option, the sanitized default computed just above is OVERWRITTEN by StringToInteger(option) with no range check. numresolution is later fed to opj_setup_encoder and controls the number of wavelet decomposition levels inside OpenJPEG, where it indexes fixed-size per-resolution arrays.

Baseline rate-control setup: one quality layer, rate[0]=0 meaning lossless, distortion allocation on. If a non-trivial quality (not 0, not 100) was requested, distoratio[0] is set to that quality and fixed-quality mode is enabled. This seeds index 0 of the two fixed-size rate arrays that the later option loops will keep writing into.

If image_info->extract is set, it is parsed as an absolute geometry into a zero-initialized RectangleInfo, and the parsed width/height/x/y become the JP2 tile dimensions and tile origin (cp_tdx/cp_tdy/cp_tx0/cp_ty0), enabling tiling. Note cp_tdx is initially set from geometry.width and cp_tdy is also first set from geometry.width (then possibly overwritten by height) — a copy-paste that would use width as the default tile height, but the values are cast to int and fed to the OpenJPEG encoder rather than used as local buffer sizes.

Parses the caller's 'jp2:quality' option as a comma-separated list of PSNR values. The loop's CONTROL expression runs sscanf(p,"%f",&parameters.tcp_distoratio[i]) FIRST — writing float element i of the fixed tcp_distoratio array — and only then does the loop body test 'if (i > 100)'. It then advances p past the next comma and repeats. On exit it records the number of parsed layers as i+1 in tcp_numlayers and turns on fixed-quality mode.

Maps the 'jp2:progression-order' option string to one of the five OpenJPEG progression-order enum values. Any unrecognized string simply leaves prog_order at its default. This is a bounded enum selection with no arithmetic and no buffer, so it carries no memory-safety contract.

Mirror of the quality loop but for the 'jp2:rate' option, writing comma-separated floats into the fixed tcp_rates array. Again the loop condition performs sscanf into tcp_rates[i] before the in-body bound test, here 'if (i >= 100)'. It sets tcp_numlayers = i+1 and enables distortion-allocation rate control.

Parses image_info->sampling_factor as two comma-separated integers into subsampling_dx/dy (chroma subsampling). The sscanf return value is discarded, so if the string parses zero or one field, dx/dy keep whatever they held (the defaults of 1, unless a subsequent branch overrides dx). Then the image 'comment' property, if present, is aliased into parameters.cp_comment (a borrowed pointer, so its lifetime must outlast the encode).

Determines the component count and colour space. Default is 3 (sRGB). YUV -> SYCC with dx forced to 2. Otherwise gray -> 1 channel/GRAY, or non-gray -> converted to sRGB; and if the image carries alpha, channels is incremented. So channels is 1, 2 (gray+alpha), 3, or 4 (sRGB+alpha) — never above 4. Multi-component transform (mct) is enabled only for exactly 3 channels.

Zeroes the five-element jp2_info array, then fills the first 'channels' entries. Each component gets precision and bits-per-pixel equal to image->depth (bumped by one for 1-bit JP2/JPT to satisfy OpenJPEG), unsigned samples, the parsed subsampling factors as dx/dy, and width/height from the image dimensions. Because channels <= 4, only jp2_info[0..3] are touched, which fits the array of 5. dx/dy here are the user-influenced subsampling values and become the component sampling used later in pixel addressing and in opj_image_create's buffer sizing.

opj_image_create allocates the OpenJPEG image object and its per-component sample buffers sized from jp2_info (w/h/dx/dy). On failure it throws. It then computes the image bounding box x1/y1 from the offset, dimensions minus one, and the (user-parsed) subsampling factor. Cinema profile compliance is applied for 12-bit DCI-sized frames. Finally the alpha component index is flagged for RGBA (comps[3]) or gray+alpha (comps[1]) — indices consistent with the channel count.

The pixel copy. For each row it fetches a scanline via GetVirtualPixels, then for each column and each channel computes scale = (2^prec - 1)/QuantumRange and a destination index into comps[i].data. The index divides y and x by the component subsampling dy/dx, so subsampling controls the divisor. The switch selects the correct source channel (gray/red, alpha/green, blue, alpha) and stores the scaled integer sample into *q. Progress is reported per row.

Selects the codec by the requested magick (JPT/J2K/JP2), installs warning/error handlers, and calls opj_setup_encoder(&parameters, jp2_image) — this is where the fully user-influenced parameters struct (numresolution, tcp_numlayers, tcp_rates/tcp_distoratio) is consumed by OpenJPEG. A stream is created with I/O callbacks bound to the ImageMagick blob, then start/encode/end compress run; any failure destroys the stream, codec and image and throws. Error paths free resources before throwing.

Success cleanup: destroy the stream, codec and image objects, close the output blob, and return MagickTrue. The teardown order and the per-error-path frees above show the authors are careful about leaks on the encode object lifetimes.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Off-by-one/two out-of-bounds write. The loop-control expression executes sscanf(p,"%f",&parameters.tcp_distoratio[i]) — writing element i of the FIXED array tcp_distoratio (declared float tcp_distoratio[100] in openjpeg.h, so valid indices are 0..99) — and only AFTER that write does the body test 'if (i > 100)'. An attacker who supplies a 'jp2:quality' option with 101+ comma-separated numeric fields drives i to 100 and 101, so tcp_distoratio[100] and tcp_distoratio[101] are written before the guard fires. Because parameters is a local opj_cparameters_t on the stack, these writes corrupt adjacent stack fields of the parameters struct — a stack out-of-bounds write (CWE-787). The correct guard must reject i before the sscanf writes (bound the index in the loop condition, e.g. i < 100), not one iteration too late.

The same defect on the parallel array. The loop condition writes tcp_rates[i] (float tcp_rates[100], indices 0..99) before the body checks 'if (i >= 100)'. When the 'jp2:rate' option contains 100+ comma-separated values, the iteration with i==100 first executes sscanf into tcp_rates[100] — one element past the array — and only then breaks. That single 4-byte out-of-bounds write past tcp_rates lands inside the same stack parameters struct (CWE-787). The 'i >= 100' guard is off by one because it is checked after the write, not before; a safe version bounds i in the loop condition.

Downstream of the two loops, tcp_numlayers is set to i+1, the count of parsed layers. If the attacker supplied ~100+ fields, tcp_numlayers becomes >= 100. opj_setup_encoder/opj_encode then iterate tcp_numlayers times over tcp_rates/tcp_distoratio inside OpenJPEG, so an inflated layer count causes out-of-bounds READS of those fixed arrays and can drive encoder-internal per-layer array indexing past its bounds (CWE-125). The implementation must cap tcp_numlayers to the array capacity.

Missing input validation on numresolution. StringToInteger parses arbitrary caller text into parameters.numresolution with no lower/upper bound, discarding the sanitized value the dimension-based loop just computed. OpenJPEG constrains the resolution count to 1..OPJ_J2K_MAXRLVLS (33); a negative value, zero, or a value larger than the image can support makes opj_setup_encoder index its fixed per-resolution level arrays out of range or compute a degenerate decomposition, leading to crashes or memory corruption inside the encoder (CWE-20 / CWE-665 improper initialization of the encoder configuration). The safe contract is to clamp numresolution to [1, OPJ_J2K_MAXRLVLS] and to what the dimensions permit before calling opj_setup_encoder.

Possible division by zero. comps[i].dx and comps[i].dy are set from parameters.subsampling_dx/dy (lines 198-199), which come from the user 'sampling_factor' string parsed by sscanf at line 160 with its return value ignored. Because opj_set_default_encoder_parameters seeds dx/dy to 1, a normal path is safe, but a caller supplying "0,0" (or a value that parses to 0) sets subsampling_dx=0; the divisions by comps[i].dx here then trigger an integer divide-by-zero and crash the process (CWE-369). The implementation must validate that subsampling factors are >= 1 before they are used as divisors.

Undefined/undersized shift. comps[i].prec derives from image->depth (jp2_info[i].prec = image->depth, possibly +1), and image->depth originates from the untrusted source image. 1UL is an unsigned long; shifting it by prec >= the bit width of unsigned long (>= 64 on LP64) is undefined behaviour, and even prec == 64 saturates the shift. A crafted image reporting a large depth (ImageMagick generally caps depth at 64) makes (1UL << prec) undefined, yielding a garbage scale factor and, at prec around 32-63, an enormous scale that overflows the (int) cast when stored to *q (CWE-190 / undefined behaviour). The implementation should bound prec to a supported sample width before the shift.

Integer overflow / underflow in the bounding-box computation. columns and rows are size_t image dimensions from the source; if either is 0, (columns-1) underflows to a huge value, and even for legitimate large dimensions multiplied by an attacker-influenced subsampling_dx the product can overflow the 32-bit unsigned result. A wrong x1/y1 disagrees with the component buffer sizes opj_image_create allocated from jp2_info.w/h, which can make the encoder read/write past those buffers (CWE-190). The implementation must reject zero dimensions and validate that the computed canvas matches the allocated component sizes.

Fixed five-element component array driven by the channels count. jp2_info[5] is written in the loop 'for (i=0; i < channels; i++)' (line 186) and its first 'channels' entries are passed to opj_image_create. In the code as shown channels is provably 1..4 (base 3, gray forces 1, alpha adds at most 1), so all indices are in range and there is no overflow here. The contract to uphold is that channels can never exceed 5; any future colour path (e.g. CMYK, extra alpha) that raised channels above 5 would overflow this stack array (CWE-787). Documented as an in-range fixed-buffer/count pairing, not currently exploitable.

summary: A JP2 encoder wrapper around OpenJPEG whose real risk is that caller-controlled option strings are parsed straight into fixed-size stack fields of opj_cparameters_t without bounds. The two comma-list loops (jp2:quality -> tcp_distoratio[], jp2:rate -> tcp_rates[]) sscanf into array[i] in the loop condition and only bound i afterward, giving an off-by-one/two out-of-bounds stack write; numresolution is taken from user text with no clamp; and user subsampling/depth values can force a divide-by-zero and an undefined shift. Most impactful in image-conversion services that let clients set write options.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point; the risk lives in the fields it parses, tracked under their own anchors.
Stack config struct; its dangerous fixed arrays tcp_rates/tcp_distoratio are tracked as separate findings.
Fixed-size buffer written under the channels count; documented in-range but contract that channels<=5 must hold.
Reused loop counter but re-seeded before each loop; no cross-loop state leak.
Provably bounded to 1..4 by the colorspace/alpha logic at 165-183; safe against jp2_info[5].
Handle checks (asserts compiled out in release) and blob open with error return; no untrusted parsing.
Loop bound i<6 clamps the derived numresolution to <=5; sane default.
User integer overrides the clamped default with no range check; feeds opj_setup_encoder.
Constant index 0 writes; the risk is the later loop growth of tcp_numlayers, tracked separately.
Parsed ints cast into cp_tdx/tdy/tx0/ty0 encoder scalars, not local buffers; cp_tdy copy-paste is a quality nit, not memory unsafety.
sscanf writes array[i] before the 'i>100' guard -> out-of-bounds stack write.
Bounded string-to-enum selection; no arithmetic or buffer.
sscanf writes array[i] before the 'i>=100' guard -> out-of-bounds stack write.
Attacker-inflated layer count drives OOB reads of the rate arrays inside the encoder.
Unchecked; a 0 factor becomes a divisor at 246-247 -> divide-by-zero.
Borrowed pointer used only during this synchronous encode; property outlives the call.
Establishes channels in 1..4 and mct; no unsafe memory operation.
In-range array fill; the depth-derived hazard surfaces at the shift (245), tracked there.
Allocates component buffers from jp2_info and NULL-checks the result; sizing hazard tracked via x1/y1 and subsampling anchors.
(columns-1)/(rows-1) underflow at 0 dims and multiply overflow into a 32-bit result.
Exact-match dimension/depth gate calling into library helper; no local memory operation.
Indices guarded by the matching channel count; consistent with allocated components.
prec from untrusted image->depth; shift >= word width is undefined.
Division by comps[i].dx/dy which can be user-set to 0 -> divide-by-zero.
Channel selection into the computed q; addressing risk is captured by the dx/dy divisor and x1/y1 anchors.
Consumes the parameters struct (risk lives in the parsed fields) and frees resources on every error path; no leak/UAF observed.
Balanced teardown of stream/codec/image and CloseBlob; correct lifetimes.

findings.title: Out-of-bounds stack write parsing jp2:quality into tcp_distoratio[]
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds stack write (off-by-one/two, guard checked after write)
findings.invariant: The index i must be verified to be within [0, capacity-1] of tcp_distoratio BEFORE sscanf writes tcp_distoratio[i]; the bound belongs in the loop condition, not one iteration late in the body.
findings.failure_mode: tcp_distoratio is a fixed float[100] inside the stack opj_cparameters_t. The loop condition writes element i, then the body checks 'i > 100', so a jp2:quality option with 101+ comma-separated fields writes tcp_distoratio[100] and [101] past the array end, corrupting adjacent stack fields of the parameters struct (potential control-flow/data corruption).
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the declared size of opj_cparameters_t.tcp_distoratio (100 in openjpeg.h) and that no earlier caller bounds the option; verify i can reach 100/101 with a long comma list.

findings.title: Out-of-bounds stack write parsing jp2:rate into tcp_rates[]
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds stack write (off-by-one, guard checked after write)
findings.invariant: i must be range-checked against the tcp_rates capacity in the loop condition, before the sscanf that writes tcp_rates[i].
findings.failure_mode: tcp_rates is a fixed float[100] in the stack parameters struct. Because the sscanf write precedes the 'i >= 100' test, an attacker supplying 100+ comma-separated jp2:rate values causes a write to tcp_rates[100] (one past the array) before the loop breaks, corrupting the neighbouring stack field.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm tcp_rates[100] declaration and that the option string length is not pre-capped; reproduce with >=100 comma-separated rates.

findings.title: Unvalidated numresolution from jp2:number-resolutions option
findings.bug_class: Missing input validation / improper initialization of encoder config
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0193_cwe665.c (WriteJP2Image) and OpenJPEG opj_setup_encoder (file not provided)
findings.invariant: numresolution must be clamped to [1, OPJ_J2K_MAXRLVLS (33)] and to a value the image dimensions can support before it reaches opj_setup_encoder.
findings.failure_mode: StringToInteger accepts arbitrary text, so a negative, zero, or very large numresolution replaces the dimension-derived default. OpenJPEG then indexes fixed per-resolution-level arrays with this count, producing out-of-range access, an assertion, or a degenerate wavelet decomposition (crash or memory corruption inside the encoder).
findings.what_to_confirm: Check whether opj_setup_encoder rejects out-of-range numresolution or whether it must be clamped by the caller; verify no clamp exists between line 67 and line 298.

findings.title: Inflated tcp_numlayers drives out-of-bounds reads of the rate arrays
findings.bug_class: Unbounded count driving out-of-bounds array read
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0193_cwe665.c (WriteJP2Image) and OpenJPEG opj_encode (file not provided)
findings.invariant: tcp_numlayers must not exceed the capacity of tcp_rates/tcp_distoratio (100) that the encoder iterates over.
findings.failure_mode: The quality and rate loops set tcp_numlayers = i+1 from the attacker-controlled field count. With ~100+ comma-separated values this exceeds the array capacity, and opj_setup_encoder/opj_encode iterate tcp_numlayers times over tcp_rates/tcp_distoratio, reading past their ends.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm how OpenJPEG bounds tcp_numlayers against the 100-entry arrays and whether the caller must cap it.

findings.title: Divide-by-zero from user subsampling factor of 0
findings.invariant: comps[i].dx and comps[i].dy (from parameters.subsampling_dx/dy) must be >= 1 before being used as divisors.
findings.failure_mode: subsampling_dx/dy are filled by the unchecked sscanf of image_info->sampling_factor (line 160), whose return value is ignored. A caller passing "0,0" (or a value parsing to 0) makes dx=0; the divisions '/jp2_image->comps[i].dx' here then perform integer division by zero and crash the process (DoS).
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify sampling_factor is not validated elsewhere and that opj_image_create does not reject dx=0; reproduce with sampling_factor="0,0".

findings.title: Undefined shift when sample precision derives from untrusted image depth
findings.invariant: comps[i].prec must be < the bit width of unsigned long (and within the supported sample size) before being used as a shift amount.
findings.failure_mode: prec is set from image->depth (line 188), which comes from the untrusted source image. If depth reaches or exceeds the width of unsigned long, '1UL << prec' is undefined; at large but sub-word values it yields an enormous scale that overflows the subsequent (int) sample cast, producing garbage samples.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the maximum image->depth ImageMagick allows to reach this coder and add a bound on prec before the shift.

findings.title: Integer underflow/overflow in JP2 canvas (x1/y1) computation
findings.invariant: image->columns and image->rows must be non-zero, and the computed canvas must equal the component buffer extents opj_image_create allocated from jp2_info.w/h.
findings.failure_mode: If columns or rows is 0, (columns-1) underflows to SIZE_MAX; combined with an attacker-influenced subsampling_dx the product can also overflow the 32-bit unsigned result. A canvas that disagrees with the allocated component buffers can lead OpenJPEG to read or write outside those buffers.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm callers reject zero-dimension images before this coder and that x1/y1 stay consistent with the allocated comps buffers.

findings.title: Fixed jp2_info[5] relies on channels never exceeding 5
findings.bug_class: Fixed-size buffer bounded by a count field (currently in range)
findings.invariant: The channels count that indexes jp2_info in the loop at line 186 must always be <= 5.
findings.failure_mode: In the code as shown channels is provably 1..4, so jp2_info[0..3] stay in bounds and there is no overflow. The contract is documented per review method because it pairs a fixed buffer with a count: any future path (CMYK, multiple alpha planes) that pushed channels above 5 would write past jp2_info on the stack.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no colorspace path can raise channels above 4 and that jp2_info retains 5 slots.

audit_checklist: Bound the jp2:quality and jp2:rate parse loops so i is checked against the tcp_distoratio/tcp_rates capacity (100) BEFORE the sscanf write, and cap tcp_numlayers to that capacity.
Clamp parameters.numresolution from jp2:number-resolutions to [1, OPJ_J2K_MAXRLVLS] and to a value the image dimensions support before opj_setup_encoder.
Validate image_info->sampling_factor: reject or default subsampling_dx/dy of 0 to prevent the divide-by-zero at the pixel-address computation.
Bound comps[i].prec (image->depth) below the machine word width before '1UL << prec' and check for overflow in the (int) sample cast.
Reject zero-width/height images and verify the x1/y1 canvas matches the component buffers opj_image_create allocated from jp2_info.w/h.
Confirm channels can never exceed the jp2_info[5] capacity across all colorspace/alpha paths.
Confirm the comment property aliased into cp_comment outlives opj_end_compress and is not freed mid-encode.
